nodes.name: Initial merge/rebase + resolve
nodes.prompt:
You are performing a git $mode against the target branch '$branch' and resolving any conflicts. MODE = '$mode'. TARGET BRANCH = '$branch'.

STEP 1: Validate. $mode must be exactly 'merge' or 'rebase'. $branch must be a real branch (verify with `git rev-parse --verify $branch`). If either is invalid, write the error to fixes-made.txt and stop.

STEP 2: Capture two baseline SHAs the reviewers will diff against. Run `git rev-parse HEAD` and write that SHA (and only the SHA, no extra text) to pre-op-sha.txt. Run `git rev-parse $branch` and write that SHA (and only the SHA, no extra text) to target-sha.txt. pre-op-sha = your branch tip before op. target-sha = target branch tip at op time, frozen so later pushes to the target do not move the goalposts mid-loop.

STEP 3: Run the operation:
  - If $mode == 'merge': run `git merge $branch --no-ff --no-edit`
  - If $mode == 'rebase': run `git rebase $branch`

STEP 4: If conflicts arise, resolve them carefully. Preserve intent from both sides. Do NOT silently delete logic. After resolving, stage the resolved files and continue (for merge: `git commit --no-edit`; for rebase: `git rebase --continue`). Repeat until the operation completes.

STEP 5: Append to fixes-made.txt a clear summary: which files had conflicts, how each was resolved, and any judgement calls made. If there were no conflicts, write that explicitly.

You run in headless mode; the user sees nothing of your output. All status info must go in fixes-made.txt. Do NOT push. Do NOT abort the operation no matter what — if you cannot resolve, leave the repo in its conflicted state and describe the blocker in fixes-made.txt; a later node decides whether to abort.

nodes.name: Claude review (regression check)
nodes.prompt:
A junior dev just performed a git $mode against branch '$branch' and resolved conflicts. MODE = '$mode'. TARGET BRANCH = '$branch'. Read pre-op-sha.txt for PRE_OP_SHA (your branch tip before the op) and target-sha.txt for TARGET_SHA (target branch tip frozen at op time). Read fixes-made.txt to see what the dev claims they did.

Diff 1 — what the op added to your side: `git diff <PRE_OP_SHA>..HEAD` plus `git diff <PRE_OP_SHA>` for any unstaged remains.

Diff 2 — verify target-branch changes survived: `git log <PRE_OP_SHA>..<TARGET_SHA>` lists every commit that should have landed in HEAD via this op. For each such commit, run `git show <commit>` and confirm its changes are present in HEAD (use `git diff <TARGET_SHA>..HEAD -- <files>` on the touched paths). Anything from <TARGET_SHA> that is missing from HEAD = the resolver dropped it during conflict resolution.

Look for: regressions on your side, silently dropped target-branch logic, conflict resolutions that picked one side when both were needed, broken imports/references, stale .md guides, accidentally reverted commits, half-merged code.

Do NOT run the code. Analyze only. Be ruthless. For each issue, suggest the fix.

Write findings to cla-review.txt. If there are NO issues, leave cla-review.txt completely empty. You run headless; only cla-review.txt is seen. An empty file means 'approved' and lets the workflow proceed.

nodes.name: Codex review (regression check)
nodes.prompt:
A junior dev just performed a git $mode against branch '$branch' and resolved conflicts. MODE = '$mode'. TARGET BRANCH = '$branch'. Read pre-op-sha.txt for PRE_OP_SHA (your branch tip before the op) and target-sha.txt for TARGET_SHA (target branch tip frozen at op time). Read fixes-made.txt to see what the dev claims they did.

Diff 1 — what the op added to your side: `git diff <PRE_OP_SHA>..HEAD` plus `git diff <PRE_OP_SHA>` for any unstaged remains.

Diff 2 — verify target-branch changes survived: `git log <PRE_OP_SHA>..<TARGET_SHA>` lists every commit that should have landed in HEAD via this op. For each such commit, run `git show <commit>` and confirm its changes are present in HEAD (use `git diff <TARGET_SHA>..HEAD -- <files>` on the touched paths). Anything from <TARGET_SHA> that is missing from HEAD = the resolver dropped it during conflict resolution.

Look for: regressions on your side, silently dropped target-branch logic, conflict resolutions that picked one side when both were needed, broken imports/references, stale .md guides, accidentally reverted commits, half-merged code.

Do NOT run the code. Analyze only. Be ruthless. For each issue, suggest the fix.

Write findings to cod-review.txt. If there are NO issues, leave cod-review.txt completely empty. You run headless; only cod-review.txt is seen. An empty file means 'approved' and lets the workflow proceed.

nodes.name: Abort-or-continue decision
nodes.prompt:
You are the gatekeeper for an automated git $mode loop against branch '$branch'. Read cla-review.txt and cod-review.txt (the two reviewers' findings) and fixes-made.txt (what the resolver did).

Decide ONE of three outcomes:

1. ABORT — the situation is unrecoverable: reviewers report fundamental incompatibility, the resolver said it could not proceed, the merge/rebase is fundamentally wrong (e.g., wrong target branch, massive regressions, or the loop has clearly diverged). Write a single line to verdict.txt explaining WHY abort. Leave cla-review.txt and cod-review.txt as-is.

2. CLEAN — both reviews report no issues (both files are already empty or contain only approval text with no actionable findings). TRUNCATE cla-review.txt and cod-review.txt to be completely empty. Leave verdict.txt empty.

3. CONTINUE — there are real, fixable issues. Leave cla-review.txt and cod-review.txt populated so the resolver can address them on the next loop iteration. Leave verdict.txt empty.

VERY IMPORTANT: only write to verdict.txt for ABORT. For CLEAN and CONTINUE, verdict.txt MUST stay empty — the downstream conditional uses verdict-empty to mean 'do not abort'. Be conservative: prefer CONTINUE over ABORT unless the loop is clearly stuck or destructive.

You run headless. Do not write status anywhere else.

nodes.name: Abort git operation
nodes.prompt:
The review loop decided to ABORT the git $mode against '$branch'. Read verdict.txt for the abort reason.

If $mode == 'merge': run `git merge --abort`.
If $mode == 'rebase': run `git rebase --abort`.

Verify the working tree is clean (or back to the pre-op state) via `git status`. Append a final note to fixes-made.txt explaining that the operation was aborted and why (copy the reason from verdict.txt).

Do NOT retry the merge/rebase. Do NOT push. The workflow ends here so the human can investigate.

nodes.name: Apply reviewer feedback
nodes.prompt:
The two reviewers found issues with your git $mode against '$branch'. Read cla-review.txt and cod-review.txt — these contain the latest review findings. Read fixes-made.txt for your prior actions.

For each issue you AGREE with: fix it in the working tree. Re-stage any modified files (do NOT commit yet — the merge or rebase commit, if any, was already made; subsequent fixes go into amended commits or new commits depending on mode). If you are mid-rebase or mid-merge with conflicts still present, resolve those.

For each issue you DISAGREE with: explain why in the summary.

Append a new dated section to fixes-made.txt: for each issue (cite reviewer + finding), state agree/disagree, what you did (or why not). Keep prior content in fixes-made.txt — append, do not overwrite.

Do NOT push. Do NOT abort. If you are stuck, say so in fixes-made.txt; the next decision node will handle it.
